skill.name: dingtalk-bridge
skill.description: DingTalk group chat bridge for Claude Code that sends messages, listens for @mentions, and can run a persistent Stream bot.
skill.summary: Bridge Claude Code workflows with DingTalk group chat messages and bot replies.
skill.author: mguozhen

security_audit.summary: Static analysis produced many false positives from documentation examples, URLs, and tests, and no prompt injection attempt was found. The main confirmed risk is the intended Stream bot behavior: DingTalk @mentions are executed by the local Claude CLI with --dangerously-skip-permissions and can be kept alive through LaunchAgent persistence. This is not confirmed malicious, but it is too risky to publish without strong warnings, permission controls, and deployment guidance.

high_findings.title: Permission-Skipping Remote Prompt Execution
high_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE: The Stream bot takes DingTalk group @mention text and runs it through the Claude CLI with --dangerously-skip-permissions. Anyone who can message the bot can cause local Claude execution in the configured work directory, which creates a high-impact remote action surface even though subprocess arguments are not shell-expanded.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The code directly passes received chat text into claude -p and explicitly includes --dangerously-skip-permissions. Documentation confirms this is the intended bot behavior, so the risk is semantic and not just a scanner pattern.

medium_findings.title: Optional LaunchAgent Persistence
medium_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE with legitimate purpose: The installer can create a macOS LaunchAgent that starts the bot automatically and keeps it running. This is documented and prompted, but it persists a high-risk bot that accepts chat-driven execution.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: LaunchAgent creation is explicit in the installer and documentation. It requires user consent, so it is not proof of malicious persistence, but it materially increases exposure when paired with remote prompt execution.

medium_findings.title: Credentialed Network Bridge to DingTalk
medium_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE with expected functionality: The skill reads DingTalk credentials from environment variables or config.json and sends authenticated requests to DingTalk API and webhook endpoints. This is necessary for the bridge, but compromise of the local process or work directory can expose app credentials and conversation metadata.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The code clearly handles app keys, app secrets, local config files, and outgoing DingTalk requests. The behavior is expected for this skill, but it is security-sensitive because it combines credentials, filesystem state, and network calls.

low_findings.title: Documentation Command Examples and Hardcoded URLs
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE for most repeated static hits: README.md and SKILL.md contain many shell snippets, URLs, and markdown examples that describe installation and usage. These examples do not execute by themselves, and the hardcoded URLs are DingTalk, Claude Code, badges, or repository links rather than hidden exfiltration endpoints.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged lines are prose or fenced examples in documentation, not runtime code. They explain the same installation and messaging workflow visible in the implementation.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptographic Algorithm Static Matches
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE: The reported weak cryptographic algorithm hits in marketplace.json, README.md, SKILL.md, and src/send.py do not correspond to cryptographic operations in the reviewed code. No evidence found of MD5, SHA1, DES, or similar weak algorithms being used for security decisions.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review found no weak cryptographic primitive at the cited locations. The hits appear to be keyword collisions in product text or message examples rather than executable crypto code.

low_findings.title: Test-Only Dynamic Import and Subprocess Mocks
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE: tests/test_dingtalk.py uses __import__ and subprocess.run patches to test timeout and bot execution behavior. These are test harness constructs and do not add runtime behavior to the installed skill.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited subprocess and __import__ usage is inside unit tests with mocks. It validates behavior instead of creating an execution path for users.

dangerous_patterns.title: Permission-Skipping Remote Prompt Execution
dangerous_patterns.description: TRUE_POSITIVE: The Stream bot takes DingTalk group @mention text and runs it through the Claude CLI with --dangerously-skip-permissions. Anyone who can message the bot can cause local Claude execution in the configured work directory, which creates a high-impact remote action surface even though subprocess arguments are not shell-expanded.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The code directly passes received chat text into claude -p and explicitly includes --dangerously-skip-permissions. Documentation confirms this is the intended bot behavior, so the risk is semantic and not just a scanner pattern.

dangerous_patterns.title: Optional LaunchAgent Persistence
dangerous_patterns.description: TRUE_POSITIVE with legitimate purpose: The installer can create a macOS LaunchAgent that starts the bot automatically and keeps it running. This is documented and prompted, but it persists a high-risk bot that accepts chat-driven execution.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: LaunchAgent creation is explicit in the installer and documentation. It requires user consent, so it is not proof of malicious persistence, but it materially increases exposure when paired with remote prompt execution.

content.value_statement: Teams need a direct way to send Claude Code updates into DingTalk. This skill adds DingTalk messaging, @mention handling, and bot replies for controlled chat workflows.
actual_capabilities: Send Markdown messages to a configured DingTalk group through OpenAPI.
Send plain text notifications from the command line or Python module.
Listen for DingTalk group @mentions with the Stream SDK.
Save conversation metadata after the first bot mention.
Run a persistent Stream bot with keepalive and retry behavior.
Reply to group messages through a webhook or DingTalk OpenAPI.
limitations: Requires a DingTalk Enterprise Internal App with robot capability enabled.
The default configuration stores one conversation target at a time.
The Stream bot runs local Claude CLI commands from incoming chat text.
The optional LaunchAgent setup is macOS-specific.

use_cases.title: Share Build and Deploy Updates
use_cases.description: Send concise status messages from Claude Code into a DingTalk engineering group.

use_cases.title: Run ChatOps Requests
use_cases.description: Let a trusted team mention the bot and receive Claude Code responses in the same group.

use_cases.title: Monitor Agent Workflows
use_cases.description: Keep a persistent DingTalk bridge online for long-running agent notifications and replies.

prompt_templates.title: Send a Basic Update
prompt_templates.prompt: Send a DingTalk message that says the staging build completed successfully.

prompt_templates.title: Post a Markdown Summary
prompt_templates.prompt: Send a DingTalk markdown message titled Daily Build Summary with test count, failures, and next action.

prompt_templates.title: Start the Stream Bot
prompt_templates.prompt: Start the DingTalk Stream bot for this project and explain what credentials and conversation setup are required.

prompt_templates.title: Design a Safe ChatOps Flow
prompt_templates.prompt: Review my DingTalk Bridge setup and propose controls for trusted groups, least privilege, credential storage, and bot runtime isolation.

output_examples.input: Send a DingTalk message: production deploy finished.
output_examples.output: The skill posts a short DingTalk group message confirming that the production deploy finished.

output_examples.input: Summarize today's test results in DingTalk markdown.
output_examples.output: The skill sends a formatted group update with a title, passing tests, failed tests, and follow-up owners.

output_examples.input: Run the bot so the team can mention it for help.
output_examples.output: The skill starts the Stream bot and waits for trusted DingTalk group mentions before replying with Claude Code output.

best_practices: Use the Stream bot only in trusted DingTalk groups with limited membership.
Store DingTalk credentials in environment variables or a permission-restricted config file.
Run the bot in a dedicated project directory with the least privileges possible.
anti_patterns: Do not expose the bot to broad or public group chats.
Do not run the Stream bot with administrator permissions.
Do not commit config.json or conversation metadata to version control.

faq.question: What does this skill connect?
faq.answer: It connects Claude Code workflows with DingTalk group chat messages and bot replies.

faq.question: Do I need DingTalk credentials?
faq.answer: Yes. You need an Enterprise Internal App with an app key, app secret, and robot capability.

faq.question: Can it send plain text and Markdown?
faq.answer: Yes. The send script supports both plain text and Markdown group messages.

faq.question: Why is this skill high risk?
faq.answer: The Stream bot can run local Claude CLI commands from DingTalk @mentions with permission checks skipped.

faq.question: Does it support multiple groups by default?
faq.answer: No. The default conversation file stores one group conversation target at a time.

faq.question: Is the LaunchAgent required?
faq.answer: No. The LaunchAgent is optional and only applies to macOS persistent bot operation.
